message:
fix(rpc/xdr): per-thread recursion-depth limit on xdr_reference (bd-xpgak)

Caller-supplied per-element procs in xdr_pointer/xdr_reference can
recursively invoke xdr_pointer themselves (e.g., generated stubs for
self-referential XDR types like `struct tree { tree *left; tree *right; }`).
A wire stream of N "more=1" flags drove N stack frames of recursion
unbounded — same DoS class as bd-lc5ss but caller-controlled. The
bd-lc5ss fix iterativized only the library-internal pmaplist; nothing
defended caller-supplied recursive types.

Add a `XDR_REFERENCE_DEPTH` thread_local Cell<u32> with a hard cap
of 256. xdr_reference increments on entry, decrements on exit, and
returns XDR_FALSE if the counter exceeds the cap. `Cell<u32>::drop`
is trivial so the thread_local does not register through
__cxa_thread_atexit_impl (avoids the bd-yf86e init-array deadlock
class). 256 is a generous practical ceiling — real RPC schemas
rarely nest more than a handful deep.

Add `xdr_reference_depth_limit_refuses_deep_recursive_wire_without_overflow`
regression test: 1024-deep recursive RecursiveNode chain. Recursive
proc would stack-overflow without the cap; the new defense rejects
with XDR_FALSE.

Verified: 90/90 xdr_abi_test pass; clippy clean.
